blocks.content: He drove past the cherry trees on Fairfield Road.

In daylight they were different than they'd been at night — less luminous, more real. Pale pink blossoms against a blue sky, the branches full in a way that wouldn't last long. A week, maybe. Maybe less, if it rained.
He slowed again, and this time pulled to the curb. He sat in the idling car and looked at them for a full minute.

He was thinking about something Mr. Alvarez had said to him, weeks ago, in the coffee shop with the morning running its course around them: that some people don't hide things to deceive you. They hide them because they are protecting something fragile. Not the secret itself — something more delicate. The version of themselves that exists when it isn't in the room.

He thought about who Cassandra was when the difficult thing wasn't in the room. How she moved. What she laughed at. The specific quality of her attention — the way she noticed things that most people went past, because she understood that things were worth noticing.
She's been living this deliberately because she understands something about time that most people learn too late, if they learn it at all.
He understood it too, now. He had learned it at a fountain, from a woman sitting on a wall.
He put the car in drive.
He drove to the Copper Mug to meet the others.
